claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your child (or children). 

**First question: How old is your child?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

If you have multiple children, we can assess each one. Let's start with the first child - what is their name and age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the **under 16** category. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is 8 years old, I expect they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this as part of the eligibility check. This requirement ensures that children who are working full-time (24+ hours per week) don't qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, Alex doesn't receive benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in their own name, correct? This is typically relevant for older teenagers, but I need to confirm for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is relevant because certain apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility, though this typically applies to older teenagers (16+). Since Alex is only 8, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're only 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is about **you** (the claimant), not Alex.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You're considered responsible if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be, through things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help with their upkeep - I contribute towards things like clothes and school dinners.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me get the Child Benefit payment amounts so we can check if your contributions meet the requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. To meet the responsibility requirement through contributions, you need to be contributing **at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay**.

Currently, the Child Benefit rates are:
- **£25.60 per week** (£110.85 per month) for the eldest or only child
- £16.95 per week (£73.45 per month) for additional children

Since Alex appears to be your only child, the threshold would be around **£25.60 per week or £110.85 per month**.

**Important questions:**

1. **Do your contributions (for things like clothes and school dinners) equal or exceed approximately £110 per month or £25-26 per week?** These contributions can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments.

2. Or, **do you actually live with Alex?** You mentioned Alex doesn't live with you, but I want to make sure - does Alex live with you for any amount of time, or do they live entirely with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute about £26 per week towards Alex's needs - so that should meet the threshold.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Your weekly contributions of £26 meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. This is important because:

- If no one else is claiming, we can continue
- If someone else is claiming but you've both agreed that you should be the claimant instead, that's fine
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision

So, is anyone else (such as Alex's other parent or another caregiver) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation, as there are special rules for certain circumstances:

**Which of the following describes Alex's current living situation?**

1. **Living normally** (at home with family/caregivers)
2. In hospital
3. In care (looked after by the local council)
4. Being fostered

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally - they're with their other parent.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the local council isn't contributing to or paying for Alex's housing or upkeep, correct? This would typically happen if a child was in care or being looked after by the local authority.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me now provide you with the eligibility outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex.

## Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex qualifies as an eligible child** - At 8 years old, Alex is under the standard maximum age of 16  
✅ **You meet the residency requirement** - You normally live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **You are responsible for Alex** - Your weekly contributions of £26 meet the requirement (the threshold is £25.60/week)  
✅ **No one else is claiming** - No other person is currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **No council involvement** - The local council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

Even though Alex doesn't live with you, your financial contributions towards their upkeep (clothes, school dinners, etc.) establish your responsibility for them under Child Benefit rules.

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through your tax return (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Do you have any questions about the application process or your eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under the standard maximum age of 16; (2) Alex is not in paid work; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets or exceeds the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week; (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally with their other parent; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite not living with Alex, the claimant meets the responsibility requirement through weekly financial contributions of £26 towards Alex's needs. The claimant lives in the UK and no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under the standard maximum age of 16; (2) Alex is not in paid work; (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which meets or exceeds the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week; (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally with their other parent; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite not living with Alex, the claimant meets the responsibility requirement through weekly financial contributions of £26 towards Alex's needs. The claimant lives in the UK and no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
